steps.title: Map the two movements
steps.content: The first sentence says Barton is fortunate not to have overheard his neighbors. The narrator then broadens from that one case to a general reflection on how painful it would be to know others' views of our actions. That shift matters because the question asks about structure, not only Barton's situation.

steps.title: Choose the structure
steps.content: A invents a resolved disagreement. C treats the second part as a prediction about Barton, but it is a generalization. D says the neighbors' negative opinion is justified, which the passage does not argue. The specific-to-general description fits. The answer is B.
